title: Red Wine on Silk
stainChemistry: Red wine tannins (polyphenolic compounds) bond to silk through hydrogen bonding and Van der Waals forces. Tannins form hydrogen bonds with amino acid side chains in silk protein. Alcohol in wine can denature silk's surface structure if too hot. Silk's delicate nature and dye sensitivity means we must avoid heat, alkali, and aggressive chemistry. Red wine is nearly pure tannin in acidic solution—acidity actually helps break tannin bonds, making this one of the few stains where acidic spotters work.
whyThisWorks: Cold water flush removes alcohol and mobilizes tannins. Acetic acid (lower pH) breaks hydrogen bonds between tannins and silk protein. Tannic acid reduction via acidic chemistry is the mechanism. Blotting prevents tannin from setting deeper. This avoids heat (which would denature silk) while using tannin's natural affinity for acid.

spottingProtocol.equipment: White towel, spray bottle, flat surface
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush cold water from back of garment. Blot from front. Work back to front. Speed matters — fresh tannin lifts easily, set tannin does not.
If color remains: apply acetic acid spotter, work briefly, blot. If residue feels sticky but color is gone: use NSD solution instead. Check and repeat if needed.
spottingProtocol.suggestedProducts: White Vinegar (acid approach) or NSD (detergent approach)

spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush with cold water until completely clear. All acid and tannin residue must be removed. Work from back to front.

aftercare: Lay flat on clean towel to air dry. Never wring silk. No heat — heat sets residual tannin. If stain remains after drying, professional treatment with commercial tannin remover is the next step.

homeSolutions.agent: Silk + red wine: speed decides the outcome
homeSolutions.instruction: Red wine is a tannin stain; silk is a protein fiber. Fresh red wine on silk, caught in the first hour, usually resolves cleanly with cold water + dilute vinegar. Anything set — dried, heat-exposed, or sitting overnight — is professional territory because silk bans the stronger tools (no enzymes, no peroxide over 45 sec, no alkaline). Your window is small. Don't panic, but don't dawdle.

homeSolutions.agent: Silk rules + tannin rules — both apply
homeSolutions.instruction: Silk: no enzymes (digest silk protein), no hot water (denatures fiber + sets tannin), no chlorine bleach, no alkaline, no wringing or scrubbing (ruptures fiber when wet). Tannin: no alkaline cleaners (turn red-purple wine permanently into dark brown). The only home tools allowed here: cold water, dilute white vinegar, silk-safe detergent (Eucalan, Soak), and VERY dilute 3% peroxide on white silk only with <45-sec dwell.

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden seam
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab a drop of the vinegar solution you'll use in step 5 on an inside seam. Wait 60 seconds, blot with a white cloth. Silk dye is fragile — if any color transfers, stop and go to step 7. Home chemistry will lighten the silk.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold water blot from the BACK — do this fast
homeSolutions.instruction: Turn the garment inside out. Place a clean white towel under the stain. Dab cold (ideally ice) water on the BACK of the stain with a cotton ball or a corner of a clean white cloth. Blot between a folded white cloth underneath — let the underside catch the runoff. Replace the underside cloth as it picks up pink. Do NOT scrub, wring, or press hard. 60 seconds of patient blotting often takes a fresh wine stain to a pale shadow.

If a pink or red shadow remains after blotting, mix 1 part white vinegar with 1 part cold water. Dip a cotton swab and press onto the shadow from the FRONT, keeping a white cloth under the back to catch transfer. 60 SECONDS MAXIMUM dwell on silk. Set a timer. Rinse with cold water from the back, blot dry between white towels. The mild acid keeps tannin in its lighter red form instead of drifting toward brown.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never apply undiluted vinegar to silk. Never leave vinegar on silk longer than 60 seconds. Never combine with bleach or peroxide.

homeSolutions.agent: Press between towels, lay flat to air-dry
homeSolutions.instruction: Lay the silk flat between two dry white towels, press gently to draw moisture out — no wringing, no twisting, no hanging wet silk (gravity stretches the fiber). Reshape while damp; silk holds its dry shape. Air-dry at room temperature out of direct sun. No heat source — no hair dryer, no iron, no dryer. Residual wine shadow + heat = permanent.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate — silk + wine has a narrow home window
homeSolutions.instruction: If the patch test failed, the wine is older than a few hours, or a shadow remains after step 5, take the garment to a cleaner who specializes in silk (not every cleaner — call first). Tell them: "Red wine on silk, age [x], treated at home with cold water + 1:1 dilute vinegar (60-sec dwell). No enzymes, no bleach, no heat. Residual [pink/brown] shadow remains." Pros use reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) with timed dwell — the specific chemistry that safely lifts oxidized tannin on silk. Not available safely for home use.

materialWarnings: NEVER use hot water—sets tannins deeper and denatures silk
NEVER use alkaline cleaners (pH>8)—weakens silk structure
NEVER use enzymes—harm silk's own protein
NEVER use strong bleach—damages dye and silk fiber
AVOID prolonged soaking—silk weakens in liquid
escalation: If wine stain is older than 6-8 hours or if color remains dark red/brown after acetic acid treatment, professional cleaner with access to tannin-specific spotters (commercial tannin removers, or reducing bleach for very stubborn oxidized stains) may help. Risk of silk damage increases with time and aggressive chemistry.

household.name: White Vinegar
household.note: Acidic spotter for tannin. Dilute 1:1 with cold water. Breaks tannin-silk bonds. DO NOT substitute industrial-strength (28%) acetic acid — it causes chemical burns.

household.name: Mild Liquid Dish Soap (dye-free, fragrance-free)
household.note: Use 1/4 tsp in 1 cup cold water. Gentle pH-neutral substitute for professional NSD. For tannin residue suspension. pH-neutral, silk-safe.

professional.name: StreeTAN (Tannin Spotter)
professional.note: Professional-grade acetic acid spotter. Stronger than 28% Acetic Acid (diluted).

customerExplanation: Red wine contains tannins—plant compounds that bond to silk through hydrogen attraction. Cold water flushes the bulk of the wine. Mild acid (vinegar) breaks the bonds between tannins and silk. Silk is delicate, so we avoid heat and strong chemistry. Speed and gentle handling are the keys.
commonMistakes: Using hot water—this sets tannins deeper. Cold only.
Waiting too long—fresh wine is much easier to remove than oxidized tannins
Using salt-based 'club soda' approach—some people use carbonated water, but plain cold water + acid works better for silk
scienceNote: Red wine tannins are polyphenolic compounds that form through oxidation of grape phenols during fermentation. Tannins have multiple hydroxyl groups (-OH) that form hydrogen bonds with amino acids on silk protein. Acidity (pH ~3.5) protonates some of these groups, weakening bonds. This is why acidic spotters (glacial acetic acid, wine, citric acid) work on tannins—they lower pH and disrupt hydrogen bonding. Heating increases tannin oxidation and actually strengthens bonding—opposite of what happens with protein stains.
callPro: If wine stain on silk is older than 8 hours and remains dark red or has begun to brown, call a professional. Tannins oxidize over time, making them harder to remove. Professional spotters (StreeTAN, BonGo) are stronger than household vinegar. Risk of silk damage with extended treatment increases.
solventNote: Professional shops: Red wine on silk responds well to immediate cold water flush, followed by acetic acid spotter (GAA diluted 1:10 in cold water) for 1-2 minutes. For aged stains, commercial tannin remover (StreeTAN, BonGo) at recommended dilution. Never exceed brief dwell times on silk. For oxidized brown stains, StreePene (reducing bleach) may help but risk of fiber damage is high. Test on hidden area first. VDS post-rinse to prevent water spotting.
